questions.question: What is the main advantage of amplitude encoding?

options.text: Exponential compression: N values in log₂(N) qubits

questions.explanation: Amplitude encoding stores N classical values as amplitudes of log₂(N) qubits, achieving exponential compression. However, the preparation circuit for arbitrary amplitudes is O(N), potentially negating this advantage.

questions.question: Why is data encoding considered a 'bottleneck' in QML?

options.text: Quantum computers can't process classical data
If encoding takes O(N) time, quantum speedup is lost
Classical computers are faster at encoding

questions.explanation: If encoding N data points requires O(N) operations, and quantum processing achieves O(polylog(N)), the total runtime is still O(N), providing no asymptotic speedup over classical algorithms.

questions.question: In angle encoding, what does each qubit typically represent?

options.text: A bit of the data index
One feature of the input data

questions.explanation: In angle encoding, each feature x_i is encoded as a rotation angle on a separate qubit: |0⟩ → Ry(x_i)|0⟩. This means one qubit per feature of the input data.

questions.question: What is data re-uploading in quantum machine learning?

options.text: Uploading data to a quantum cloud service
Re-encoding the same data multiple times interspersed with trainable layers
Copying quantum data to multiple registers

questions.explanation: Data re-uploading encodes the input data multiple times within the circuit, with trainable variational layers between encodings. This increases the expressiveness of quantum models.

questions.question: How many qubits are needed for basis encoding of values from 0 to 255?
questions.explanation: Basis encoding represents integer i as |i⟩ in the computational basis. To represent 0-255 (256 values), we need log₂(256) = 8 qubits.

questions.question: What does the ZZ feature map include that simple angle encoding does not?

options.text: Entangling gates that encode feature interactions

questions.explanation: The ZZ feature map includes controlled rotations (like CZ or CNOT) that encode products of features (x_i × x_j), capturing interactions between features that simple angle encoding misses.

questions.question: Amplitude encoding always provides a speedup for quantum machine learning algorithms.
questions.explanation: False. While amplitude encoding compresses N values into log(N) qubits, preparing arbitrary amplitude states requires O(N) gates. This preparation cost typically eliminates any asymptotic speedup.
